hook: Sealed WWII air-raid tunnels beneath a Victorian park
facts: Dug from December 1941 as air-raid shelters; over 3.5 km of tunnels with nine entrances reached from Constitution Hill to Wellesley Street.
The expected raids never came; by 1943 timber props were failing.
Back-filled with 8.8 million unfired clay blocks and the entrances sealed by April 1946.
Renewed interest in the 1990s from a tourism promoter and architecture students.
legend: Stories tell of sealed chambers holding decommissioned weapons and 1950s rations.
Rumours of a secret passage to the government building and a trapdoor under the band rotunda.
w: Under the bandstand and the flowerbeds, miners cut three and a half kilometres of air-raid shelter in 1941, nine mouths from Constitution Hill to Wellesley Street. The bombers never came, the timbers rotted, and the council stuffed it all with 8.8 million clay bricks. They say chambers and rations still wait down there, sealed.

hook: Headland honeycombed with old gun tunnels and a famous conspiracy
facts: Volcanic headland at the harbour mouth, fortified during the Russian Scare and both World Wars.
Tunnels, magazines and disappearing-gun emplacements riddle the cone.
Co-managed today as a tupuna maunga of Tamaki Makaurau.
A 1990s court case dismissed claims of a hidden underground city as unproven.
legend: A persistent legend holds that two 1918 Boeing flying boats lie bricked up in sealed tunnels.
Believers say Boeing would pay a fortune to recover the first aircraft it ever built.
w: The harbour-mouth cone got fortified against Russians, then Germans, then Japanese, leaving it riddled with gun tunnels and magazines. The good part: a legend that two 1918 Boeing flying boats sit bricked behind a sealed wall down there. A judge ruled the hidden city unproven. Diggers keep tapping the rock anyway.

hook: Auckland's first cemetery, sliced by a motorway
facts: In use since 1842; Anglican, Catholic, Jewish, Presbyterian, Wesleyan and general sections on Grafton Gully's slopes.
Governor William Hobson, signatory of the Treaty of Waitangi, lies here.
1960s Southern Motorway works reinterred over 4,100 bodies into two memorial vaults, shrinking the ground by a quarter.
Around 80 Jewish headstones carry both English and Hebrew.
legend: Locals say some old gravestones were broken up and used as motorway hardfill.
w: The town's first burial ground, open from 1842, holds Governor Hobson and Auckland's earliest colonists down a wooded gully. Then the motorway came: in the 1960s diggers lifted over four thousand bodies into two memorial vaults. They say some old headstones ended up as roadbed fill. Traffic roars below the graves now.

hook: Once the world's largest concrete arch, with a dark reputation
facts: Opened 28 April 1910 as the largest reinforced-concrete arch span in the world, about 98 metres.
Built by the Ferro-Concrete Company of Australasia, rising some 43 metres over the gully.
Steel anti-suicide barriers were fitted in 1937, removed in 1996, then replaced after a fivefold rise in deaths.
Glass and perspex screens went up in 2002-03.
legend: Long known as the 'suicide bridge', its grim folklore shadowed it for decades.
w: When it opened in 1910 this pale arch leapt the Grafton gully as the biggest reinforced-concrete span on earth, ninety-eight metres of engineering swagger. It also drew a grim reputation; barriers went up in 1937, came down in 1996, and deaths jumped fivefold before glass screens returned. Beauty and a long shadow, both.

hook: An atmospheric picture palace with a starry indoor sky
facts: Opened 20 December 1929, the largest surviving atmospheric cinema in Australasia.
The auditorium ceiling mimics a night sky, its twinkling stars set to Auckland's of 20 April 1929.
The Indian-Persian foyer holds seated Buddhas, twisted columns and over 400 plaster elephants.
Restored over two years and reopened on its 70th birthday in 1999.
w: Step inside and the ceiling becomes a night sky, stars set to the Auckland heavens of April 1929, drifting clouds projected over a Persian fantasy of minarets. The foyer guards seated Buddhas, twisted columns and four hundred plaster elephants. Australasia's grandest atmospheric cinema, dimming the city the moment you cross the threshold.

hook: A gully park hiding a marble Moses below K Road
facts: Opened 1915 on a deteriorating gully after brewer Arthur Myers gifted 9,000 pounds to buy the land.
A full-scale marble copy of Michelangelo's Moses, quarried from the same Italian stone, stands at the foot of the steps.
Milne and Choyce department store imported it for their 1971 centenary; the city kept Moses but declined the matching David.
Steps climb to St Kevins Arcade and Karangahape Road.
w: A brewer rescued this gully for the city in 1915, planting a green slot below the bustle of K Road. At the foot of the steps sits a marble Moses, full-size, cut from the same Italian quarry as Michelangelo's. A department store shipped him over in 1971; the council took the prophet but turned down the matching David.

hook: A 1920s Neo-Greek arcade opening onto a park view
facts: Built 1924 and extended 1926 in a 1920s Neo-Greek style, originally tea rooms.
Designed by architect William Arthur Cumming as a grand entrance from K Road down to Myers Park.
Leadlight windows, cornicing and original signage survive above the shopfronts.
Turned 100 in 2024, still trading on a fading-grand strip.
w: Built in 1924 as tea rooms, this Neo-Greek arcade frames the great trick of K Road: walk through the shops, past leadlight windows and old cornicing, and the far end opens onto a balcony hung over Myers Park's green gully. A century of cafes and characters later it still trades on faded grandeur and that sudden view.

hook: A summit obelisk, an empty pedestal and a felled tree
facts: John Logan Campbell planted a Monterey pine on the tihi after a settler felled the original tree in 1852.
The 21-metre basalt and granite obelisk, bequeathed by Campbell as a memorial to Maori, was completed in 1948.
Campbell is buried on the summit; the Maori King Koroki unveiled the monument.
Maori activists chainsawed the lone pine in 1994 and 2000; it was removed in 2000, leaving the hill treeless.
w: The name promises a tree, but the summit stands bare. A settler cut the first one in 1852; Campbell's replacement pine was chainsawed by activists in 1994, again in 2000, and finally removed. What remains: a 21-metre obelisk Campbell left as a memorial to the Maori he wrongly thought would vanish, and his own grave beneath it.

hook: Auckland's oldest house, twice relocated
facts: Built June 1841 on Shortland Street for John Logan Campbell and William Brown, Auckland's oldest surviving residential building.
Campbell fitted out the interior himself over the winter nights of 1841.
Moved to Cornwall Park in 1920, then to its present spot in 1956.
A Category 1 Historic Place.
w: Auckland's oldest house began life on Shortland Street in 1841, knocked together by two young traders, Campbell doing the joinery himself through the winter nights. The city grew up around it and pushed it out: carted to Cornwall Park in 1920, shifted again in 1956. A four-room timber survivor, now grazed around by sheep.

hook: Twin glasshouses and a fernery in an old quarry
facts: Cool House built 1916-21; tropical house, fernery and courtyard added by 1928.
Designed by leading architects Gummer and Ford, the glasshouses are barrel-vaulted steel and glass.
Profits from the 1913-14 Auckland Exhibition funded the scheme.
The fernery sits in a former scoria quarry cut into the cone of Pukekaroro.
w: Two barrel-vaulted glasshouses face each other across a sunken courtyard, paid for by the profits of a 1913 trade fair and drawn up by the city's smartest architects. Slip behind them into the fernery, planted in an old scoria quarry gouged from a small volcano. Hot, dripping, prehistoric green under a Victorian roof.

hook: A Carpenter Gothic mansion built for 21 children
facts: Begun 1862 and enlarged into the 1880s, a rare Carpenter Gothic timber house in New Zealand.
Built by wool trader and auctioneer Alfred Buckland, who fathered 21 children across two marriages.
Copied from American A. J. Downing's pattern book of country houses.
Occupied by Buckland descendants until the late 1970s, now a Category 1 house museum.
w: Gabled, bargeboarded and steep-roofed, this timber house came straight out of an American pattern book, planted in Auckland by a wool auctioneer with two wives and twenty-one children to house. It kept growing as the family did. Buckland's descendants lived here until the late 1970s; the rooms still hold their furniture and clutter.

hook: A kauri parsonage frozen with a clergyman's hoard
facts: Built 1863-64 in kauri by Reverend Vicesimus Lush so his sons could attend a Parnell grammar school.
Named after a house near Oxford owned by an aunt of Lush's first wife.
The Lush family owned it for over a century, selling to the council in 1969.
Holds nearly 2,000 books, sheet music, artworks and everyday colonial objects.
legend: It carries a reputation as one of the city's more haunted houses.
w: A Victorian clergyman built this kauri cottage in 1863 so his boys could reach a Parnell school, and named it for an English house he loved. The Lush family then never really left, hoarding two thousand books, sheet music and household oddments across a century. Walk in and it reads like the parson stepped out for the afternoon.

hook: A Victorian mining depot turned navy museum
facts: Housed in 1896 buildings built to control electro-contact mines across the harbour mouth.
The site began in 1885 as a submarine mining depot during the Russian Scare.
Three lines of mines could be laid and detonated from a control room, though probably none were ever set.
The Royal New Zealand Navy's museum moved here in 2010.
w: In the 1880s, fearing Russian warships, engineers rigged the harbour mouth with lines of electric mines, fired from a control room in these brick sheds at the water's edge. The threat evaporated; probably not a single mine was ever laid. The navy's museum fills the old depot now, beside a cafe and the lapping channel.

hook: Auckland's highest cone and a sacred bowl you mustn't enter
facts: At 196 metres, the highest of Auckland's volcanic cones, with a crater 50 metres deep.
The crater, Te Ipu Kai a Mataaho, is wahi tapu and closed; visitors walk only the rim.
Terraces and defensive pa earthworks from centuries of Maori occupation ring the flanks.
Co-managed as one of 14 tupuna maunga under a Treaty settlement.
legend: Tradition names the crater the food bowl of Mataaho, the deity of things hidden in the ground.
w: The city's highest cone drops into a perfect grassed crater fifty metres deep, and you are asked not to enter it. Tradition names it Te Ipu Kai a Mataaho, the food bowl of the god of things buried in the earth, sacred and off-limits. Walk the rim instead, over the old pa terraces, with all of Auckland spread below.

hook: A cone of pa terraces, gun bunkers and a signalman's house
facts: The highest volcano on the North Shore at 66 metres, an important pa for Tamaki Maori.
Seasonally held by Waiohua chief Kiwi Tamaki for bird catching and preserving.
A signal station went up in 1841; nearby houses were built to read its flags.
Later crowned with a gun fort and concrete army bunkers.
w: Storage pits and pa terraces still corrugate the northern slope, dug by people who held this cone for the bird-catching seasons. From 1841 a flagstaff up top signalled ships, and Devonport streets were laid out to keep a clear sightline to read the flags. Gun forts and concrete bunkers came later. A whole harbour's worth of watching.

hook: A Raj-inspired mansion of verandahs and towers
facts: Began in 1863 as a plain farmhouse, transformed in the 1870s into an Anglo-Indian country manor.
Built for Allan Kerr Taylor, reworked by architect Matthew Henderson with grand verandahs and towers.
Centrepiece of a 500-acre estate on the slopes of Mount Albert.
Gifted to the nation in 1972, full of original wallpapers, furniture and family pieces.
legend: Counted among Auckland's reputedly haunted houses.
w: A modest 1863 farmhouse that a landowner reworked, in homage to the India of his birth, into a thing of deep verandahs and pointed towers at the heart of a five-hundred-acre estate. The Kerr Taylor family kept it for generations before gifting it in 1972, wallpapers, furniture and ghost stories intact. Faded grandeur, fully furnished.

hook: Five thousand roses above the harbour, with a heritage corner
facts: Officially Dove-Myer Robinson Park, named in 1981 for Auckland's longest-serving mayor.
Ratepayers voted in 1914 to borrow money and buy the land for a park.
Over 5,000 roses bloom across summer and autumn.
The 1984 Nancy Steen Garden honours a rosarian with a collection of heritage roses.
w: Ratepayers voted in 1914 to buy this headland for a park, and it now carries five thousand roses tumbling toward the Waitemata. Slip into the quieter corner, the Nancy Steen garden, where old varieties bred long before the show-bench fashions perfume the air. Named for a mayor, ruled by a rosarian's heritage stock.

hook: A brick cruciform fort from the New Zealand Wars
facts: Built 1860 as a defence strongpoint during the New Zealand Wars, cruciform with 11-inch brick walls.
Thought to be the only surviving blockhouse in the country built in brick by a public body.
Doors plated with sheet iron; sash windows let in light.
Stands in Jellicoe Park, renamed in 1921 and restored in the 1960s.
w: Built in 1860 against the fear that townsfolk's wooden homes would burn, this little cross-shaped fort went up in brick eleven inches thick, its doors sheathed in iron. They reckon it the only public brick blockhouse left in the country. It saw no battle and now sits primly in a suburban park, loopholes and all.

hook: A volcano-side planetarium born from a stargazing bequest
facts: Stands in the One Tree Hill Domain below the cone of Maungakiekie.
Fundraising began in 1948; a bequest from Edith Winstone Blackwell bought the main telescope.
The observatory opened in March 1967 on a long lease from the borough council.
A new 11-metre domed planetarium opened in 1997, the most advanced in New Zealand.
w: A widow's bequest bought the telescope, and stargazers raised the rest, opening this little observatory in 1967 on the flank of an old volcano. The reclining seats under the domed planetarium throw the southern sky overhead while the city's glow drowns the real one outside. Astronomy in the lee of a thousand-year-old cone.

hook: A sunken fort with disappearing guns and a dry moat
facts: Built 1886-89 against the Russian threat, the last New Zealand-designed twin 6-inch gun fort.
Set below ground level and ringed by a dry moat against land attack.
Twin disappearing guns, an underground magazine and barracks survive.
Searchlights swept the gulf nightly from 1899 to the end of WWII, its guns in continuous service.
w: Sunk into the clifftop above Narrow Neck, this fort hides below the grass behind a dry moat, its guns built to rise, fire and drop back out of sight. Raised in the 1880s against Russians who never sailed, its searchlights still swept the gulf every night until 1945. The last of its kind, quietly subterranean.
